blurb: The ultimate South African fridge tart — layers of coconut biscuits, caramel condensed milk, whipped cream and crushed Peppermint Crisp.

prov.popular_in: Every South African celebration and household fridge
prov.famous_for: The nostalgic combination of Peppermint Crisp chocolate and caramel that defines South African childhoods
prov.consists_of: Coconut biscuits layered with whipped cream mixed with caramel condensed milk, topped with crushed Peppermint Crisp bars
prov.name_origin: Named after the Nestlé Peppermint Crisp chocolate bar that defines it; a mid-20th-century South African invention
prov.why_loved: Cool, creamy, minty and crunchy — no baking required and always a crowd-pleaser
prov.method_detail: No-bake — biscuits layered in a dish, whipped cream mixed with caramel condensed milk, spread over biscuits, topped with crushed Peppermint Crisp, chilled 6 hours, served cold

brief.lead: A beloved South African no-bake dessert that layers coconut biscuits with a creamy caramel filling and finishes with crunchy peppermint chocolate. Perfect for celebrations and easy enough for any home cook.
expect: Assembly takes 20 minutes — no baking required
Needs 6 hours to chill — perfect for planning ahead
Serves 8-12 — ideal for parties and gatherings
The taste of South African childhood celebrations

steps: Prepare the base
Arrange Tennis biscuits in a single layer in the bottom of a 20×20cm dish, breaking some to fit if needed. Drizzle melted butter over biscuits.
Butter helps the base set and adds richness.
Make the filling
Whip the cream until stiff peaks form. Fold in caramel condensed milk and vanilla essence until smooth and combined.
The folding keeps the mixture light and fluffy.
Assemble the tart
Spread the cream-caramel mixture evenly over the biscuit base. Smooth the top with a spatula.
Even layers ensure consistent texture throughout.
Add the topping
Crush 2 Peppermint Crisp bars and sprinkle evenly over the filling. Crush the third bar more finely for decoration.
The crushed chocolate adds the signature minty crunch.
Chill thoroughly
Refrigerate for at least 6 hours, preferably overnight, until set.
Chilling allows the flavours to meld and the tart to set properly.
Serve
Cut into squares and serve cold. Store leftovers in the fridge.
Best served chilled for the ultimate creamy texture.

blurb: Afrikaans tartlets — buttery pastry filled with apricot jam and topped with fluffy coconut meringue.

prov.famous_for: The winning trio of buttery pastry, sweet apricot jam and coconut-sprinkled meringue
prov.consists_of: Shortcrust pastry shells filled with apricot jam, topped with coconut meringue and baked until golden
prov.name_origin: Named after J.B.M. Hertzog, a South African Prime Minister; created in the 1920s to honour him
prov.why_loved: Sweet, buttery and texturally perfect — the pride of Afrikaans tea-time
prov.method_detail: Oven — shortcrust pastry baked blind 10–12 min at 180°C, filled with apricot jam, topped with coconut meringue, baked 12–15 min until golden

brief.lead: A classic Afrikaans tea-time treat featuring buttery shortcrust pastry shells filled with sweet apricot jam and topped with fluffy coconut meringue. Named after a former South African Prime Minister, these tartlets are a beloved celebration of Afrikaans heritage.
expect: Blind bake first, then add filling
Best served with rooibos or Ceylon tea

steps: Make the pastry
Rub cold butter into flour and salt until mixture resembles breadcrumbs. Add ice water and mix until dough just comes together. Shape into a disc, wrap and chill for 15 minutes.
Cold butter and chilling prevent shrinkage during baking.
Prepare the tart shells
Roll out pastry to 3mm thickness. Cut circles to fit a 12-hole muffin tin. Press pastry into tins, trimming edges. Prick bases with a fork and chill 10 minutes.
Chilling again helps pastry hold its shape.
Blind bake
Line pastry shells with baking paper and fill with baking beans. Bake at 180°C for 10–12 minutes until lightly golden. Remove paper and beans.
Blind baking ensures the pastry is fully cooked.
Fill with jam
Spoon a generous teaspoon of apricot jam into each hot pastry shell. Spread slightly to cover the bottom.
The hot pastry helps the jam settle evenly.
Make the meringue
Whisk egg whites until soft peaks form. Gradually add sugar, whisking until glossy and stiff. Fold in vanilla essence and coconut.
Stiff peaks ensure the meringue holds its shape.
Top the tartlets
Spoon meringue over each jam-filled shell, spreading to seal edges. Sprinkle with extra coconut if desired.
Sealing edges prevents jam from bubbling over.
Bake until golden
Return to oven at 180°C for 12–15 minutes until meringue is lightly golden and set.
Watch carefully — coconut can brown quickly.
Cool and serve
Cool in tin for 5 minutes, then carefully transfer to wire rack. Serve warm or at room temperature with tea.
Cooling in the tin helps set the meringue.

blurb: Morocco's fragrant stew — tender lamb with dried apricots, almonds, and aromatic spices.

prov.popular_in: Moroccan households and North African restaurants
prov.famous_for: The classic Moroccan tagine with lamb, dried fruit, and nuts in spiced sauce
prov.consists_of: Lamb shoulder slowly cooked with onions, spices (cumin, coriander, cinnamon), dried apricots, almonds
prov.name_origin: Tagine = both the conical cooking vessel and the stew; Moroccan Berber tradition
prov.why_loved: The sweet-savoury combination of tender lamb, sweet apricots, and crunchy almonds with warm spices
prov.method_detail: Stovetop — lamb browned, onions slowly caramelised, spices, apricots, almonds added, stock, simmered 90-120 min until tender, garnished with almonds

brief.lead: A classic Moroccan slow-cooked stew featuring tender lamb shoulder, sweet dried apricots, and crunchy almonds in a richly spiced sauce. The perfect balance of sweet and savoury, this tagine brings the flavours of North Africa to your table.
expect: 2 hours of gentle simmering for meltingly tender lamb
Cumin, coriander, cinnamon and saffron create depth
Everything cooks together for layered flavours

steps: Prep the lamb
Cut lamb shoulder into 4cm cubes, trimming excess fat. Pat dry with paper towel.
Dry meat browns better for deeper flavour.
Brown the lamb
Heat olive oil in a heavy-based pot over medium-high heat. Season lamb with salt and pepper. Brown in batches — don't overcrowd the pan. Set aside.
Batch browning prevents steaming and develops colour.
Caramelize onions
Reduce heat to medium. Add onions to the pot with a pinch of salt. Cook slowly for 10–12 minutes until golden and soft, scraping up the browned bits from the bottom.
Caramelized onions provide the flavour base.
Add aromatics and spices
Add garlic, cumin, coriander, ginger, cinnamon stick and saffron. Cook for 1 minute until fragrant, stirring constantly.
Blooming spices releases their essential oils.
Combine everything
Return lamb to the pot. Add apricots, blanched almonds, stock and honey. Stir to combine. Bring to a simmer, then reduce heat to low.
Honey balances the tartness of apricots.
Slow cook
Cover and simmer gently for 90–120 minutes until lamb is meltingly tender. Check occasionally and add a splash of water if needed.
Slow cooking breaks down connective tissue.
Finish and serve
Remove cinnamon stick. Stir in preserved lemon if using. Taste and adjust seasoning. Sprinkle with toasted almonds. Serve with couscous or flatbread.
Toasted almonds add essential crunch.
